title: Origami Waterbomb Base
subtitle: The preliminary fold using mountain and valley folds that precedes the balloon, fish, and other models
excerpt: The waterbomb base is a flat triangular form produced from a single square using two mountain folds and two valley folds. It is the starting point for the traditional paper balloon, the fish base, and many other origami models.
sourceNotes: Kindergarten Gifts and Occupations literature, Froebel tradition; the waterbomb base is a canonical preliminary fold documented in pre-1928 literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preliminary origami form: a flat equilateral-triangle-like shape with four open flaps, produced by collapsing a square along two mountain folds and two valley folds.
A standard starting configuration in origami that sets up the crease structure from which many different models can be developed.
The step in origami where multiple pre-scored crease lines are brought together simultaneously so the paper shifts from a flat open sheet into a three-dimensional or multi-layered folded form.

content.text: What the waterbomb base is used for

: a standard starting shape that appears at the beginning of many different models. The traditional water balloon starts here. So does the fish base, several modular forms, and the inflatable cube. Learning to produce a clean waterbomb base is useful across a wide range of origami practice.

The base has four open flaps: two on each side of the central crease. Those flaps are the raw material for whatever model follows. The quality of the finished model depends on the accuracy of these five steps.

steps.caption: Place the square colour-side up. Fold in half from left to right, bringing the left edge to the right edge. Crease firmly with the bone folder along the full length of the fold. Unfold. This is a mountain fold: the crease ridge points upward when the paper is flat.

Fold in half from top to bottom, bringing the top edge down to the bottom edge. Crease firmly. Unfold. This is the second mountain fold. You now have a cross of mountain-fold creases dividing the square into four equal rectangles.
Turn the square over (colour-side down). Fold in half diagonally, bringing the bottom-left corner to the top-right corner. Crease firmly. Unfold. This is a valley fold on the colour-up face.

Fold in half on the other diagonal, bringing the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner. Crease firmly. Unfold. You now have four crease lines: two mountain folds along the medians and two valley folds along the diagonals. All four lines cross at the exact centre of the square.

Collapse the base: hold the square at the midpoints of the left and right edges. Push both sides toward the centre. The top and bottom will rise as the two mountain-fold creases on the medians fold inward and the two diagonal valley folds push the layers together. Allow the square to collapse into a flat triangle with four layers. Arrange the layers so two flaps point to the left and two to the right. Press all edges flat with the bone folder.

content.text: in Step 5 is the step that trips up beginners. The paper has to move in four directions at once, which feels counterintuitive. The key is to hold the midpoints of two opposite edges and push them together: the diagonal creases will automatically gather the top and bottom layers into the correct triangle shape. Do not try to force the shape from the top down or the corners will bunch up unevenly.

Once the triangle is formed, check that the closed point is at the top. The two open flaps on the left side should mirror the two on the right. If the layers are uneven, open and re-collapse with the midpoints held more firmly.

For the traditional water balloon, fold the free corner of each flap up to the top point of the triangle. Then fold those flap tips inward to make the inflation pockets. Finally, blow into the small hole at the base to inflate the balloon.
For a modular star, combine several waterbomb bases. Tuck the point of one base into the open pocket of the next. Work around in a circle to build a multi-pointed star with no glue needed.

items.symptom: The collapse produces a triangular form but the layers are uneven
items.cause: The mountain and valley folds were not scored firmly enough before collapsing
items.fix: Open flat and run the bone folder firmly along every crease again. The creases should hold their direction without being pushed; if they spring open, they need more pressure.

items.symptom: The base has a ridge down the middle after collapsing
items.cause: The median and diagonal creases are in the wrong direction on one fold
items.fix: Check fold direction: the two median folds (vertical and horizontal) should be mountain folds (ridge upward when flat). The two diagonal folds should be valley folds (ridge downward when flat). Reverse any that are wrong.

items.symptom: The four flaps are not equal in size
items.cause: The original square was not exactly square
items.fix: Trim the kami to an exact square using a metal ruler and craft knife before folding. Check by folding corner to opposite corner: the edges should align exactly.
